summary: In a brief excerpt from an interview with Eric Topol, Aviv Regev discusses the origins of the Human Cell Atlas. She emphasizes the necessary shift from bespoke, haphazard cell characterization to a systematic, shared approach enabled by single-cell and spatial genomics, which provides the massive datasets needed to build foundational AI models of cells.

principles.statement: Biology requires a systematic, shared approach to characterizing cells rather than niche, bespoke methods.
principles.rationale: Bespoke methods do not unify together, leaving biologists with a haphazard and fragmented view of cells despite decades of research.
principles.source_quote: ...now there would be a systematic way, like a shared way of doing it across all cells in the human body rather than in ways that were niche and bespoke and as a result didn't unify together.

principles.statement: Large-scale biological atlases are the prerequisite for building foundational computational models of biology.
principles.rationale: A unified dataset of massive magnitude and scale is required to train computational models that can truly understand cellular behavior.
principles.source_quote: ...having a map like this, an atlas as we call it, a data set of this magnitude and scale, would really allow us to build a model to understand cells. Today, we call them foundational models or foundation models.

mental_models.name: The Biological Atlas as a Foundation Model
mental_models.description: Viewing a comprehensive, systematic dataset of human cells not merely as a reference catalog, but as the essential training data required to build computational models that fundamentally 'understand' cellular biology.
mental_models.example: Creating the Human Cell Atlas using single-cell and spatial genomics to generate a dataset large enough to train what are now called foundational AI models.

signature_quotes.text: ...now there would be a systematic way, like a shared way of doing it across all cells in the human body rather than in ways that were niche and bespoke and as a result didn't unify together.
signature_quotes.context: Discussing the technological shift that enabled the Human Cell Atlas.

signature_quotes.text: ...having a map like this, an atlas as we call it, a data set of this magnitude and scale, would really allow us to build a model to understand cells. Today, we call them foundational models or foundation models.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining the computational foresight behind building the Human Cell Atlas.

anti_patterns.description: Characterizing cells using niche and bespoke methods.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It results in fragmented data that doesn't unify together, leaving scientists with a haphazard view of cellular biology.
